We prove unconditionally (i) a matching upper bound L = O �min(k,⌈k/s⌉·⌈log2(2s)⌉)·⌈log2 n/(mp)⌉  via windowed pointer doubling, and (ii) the max-bound L = Ω �max(⌈k/s⌉,⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉)  on the full parameter range; closing the conjecture amounts to upgrading max to a product in the s ≤√n/4 regime. (2) Bandwidth barrier. The product bound is quantitatively binding only in the narrow or low-precision regime (Hmp ≲logn). We formalize this limitation as a barrier theorem: any depth lower bound provable via per-window distinguishability counting—a class that includes reachability arguments, bandwidth arguments, and their combinations—cannot exceed ⌈k/s⌉in the Hmp ≥log2 n regime. Breaking this barrier would require fundamentally different proof techniques, e.g., unconditional communication- complexity lower bounds for pointer chasing. We view this as a structural obstacle on the road to characterizing depth in standard LLM configurations. (3) Sharp error behavior and adaptive–oblivious separation. Under random cache selection, we establish a two-regime characterization of joint success probability over T = ⌈log2 k⌉stages of windowed pointer doubling: with oblivious caches, Pr[E ] ≤(s/(n−T))T +2T 3/n (exponential decay in T); with adaptive locality-respecting caches, Pr[E ] = s/n exactly (matching upper and lower bounds), independent of T. The resulting Ω((n/s)T−1) separation quantifies why heavy-hitter eviction qualitatively dominates random eviction for multi-hop reasoning (Liu et al., 2025; Ananthanarayanan et al., 2026). Together, these results give a sharp picture of what current proof techniques can and cannot establish for cache-constrained reasoning. The central open problem is to close the gap between the unconditional max-bound and the conjectured product-bound; we identify the specific technical step required and survey candidate routes for closure. 1 Introduction As context windows in large language models scale beyond 100K tokens, the key–value (KV) cache has become the dominant memory bottleneck during inference (Shi et al., 2024). This has driven extensive work on cache compression through eviction (Zhang et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024; Cai et al., 2024), quantization (Liu et al., 2024), and merging (Wang et al., 2024); these methods report 80–90% compression with limited degradation on standard benchmarks. 1 arXiv:2604.17935v1 [cs.LG] 20 Apr 2026 Yet mounting empirical evidence shows that multi-hop reasoning degrades far faster than single-hop retrieval under the same compression rates (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2026; Liu et al., 2025; Cai et al., 2025). This raises a sharp question: how much cache must be retained for multi-step reasoning to remain possible? We formalize this through k-hop pointer chasing and study the depth required to solve it under a shared KV cache of size s. Our main positive result is an unconditional max-bound lower bound, plus a conjectured product lower bound: cache reachability (∼k/s sequential windows) and per-window bandwidth (∼logn/(Hmp) layers per window) should multiply rather than compete. We identify the precise probabilistic step (⋆) needed to turn the conjecture into a theorem and leave it as Open Problem 1; we also give a narrow unconditional partial result in the extreme low-precision subregime s·2mp < 8 under an additional query-only controller assumption (Remark 4.13). Our other main results (the matching upper bound, the bandwidth barrier, and the adaptive-vs-oblivious error analysis) are unconditional. The product bound is, however, quantitatively binding only in a specific parameter regime—when precision is low or models are narrow enough that Hmp ≲logn. In standard LLM configurations (H = 32, m = 128, p = 16, giving Hmp ∼6.5·104 ≫logn), the bandwidth factor ceilings at 1 and the bound reduces to the cache-reachability constraint L ≥⌈k/s⌉alone. This raises the natural question: can the product structure be sharpened to bind in the standard LLM regime? Our second contribution answers: not via a broad class of current proof techniques. We formalize a barrier theorem showing that any depth lower bound provable via per-window distinguishability counting—a class that encompasses reachability arguments, bandwidth counting, and their combinations—saturates at ⌈k/s⌉ whenever Hmp ≥log2 n. The barrier is structural: multi-head attention (H ≥2) makes per-layer bandwidth (Hmp) exceed per-token state capacity (mp), so any counting-based per-window argument ceilings within a single layer. Breaking this barrier would require either translating unconditional communication-complexity lower bounds for pointer chasing (Nisan and Wigderson, 1993; Yehudayoff, 2020; Mao et al., 2025) into Cache-Transformer depth lower bounds without the MPC conjecture (an open problem) or genuinely different proof techniques. Our third contribution is a sharp, two-regime analysis of error behavior under random cache selec- tion, which partially explains the empirical “hallucination cliff” of Ananthanarayanan et al. (2026). The joint success probability of windowed pointer doubling over T = ⌈log2 k⌉stages admits qualitatively dif- ferent scaling in two regimes: for oblivious caches (chosen without seeing π), direct enumeration gives Pr[E ] ≤(s/(n −T))T + 2T 3/n, exhibiting exponential decay in T (Theorem 3.8). For adaptive locality- respecting caches, the joint probability is exactly s/n, independent of T, and is matched by an explicit “chain-tracking” controller (Theorem 3.9). The resulting separation, Ω((n/s)T−1) between adaptive and oblivious, quantitatively explains the empirical observation (Liu et al., 2025) that heavy-hitter (adaptive) eviction qualitatively outperforms random or recency-based eviction on multi-hop reasoning tasks. Contributions. 1. Product lower bound (Conjecture 3.1): We conjecture that for any locality-respecting controller, L = Ω(⌈k/s⌉·⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉), binding in the narrow-model regime. We provide (i) an unconditional max- bound L = Ω(max(⌈k/s⌉,⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉)) from reachability and output bandwidth, (ii) an identified proof strategy via exit-based distinguishability counting, reducing the conjecture to a probabilistic step on the joint distribution of cache trace and pointer chain, and (iii) partial progress closing the gap in sub-regimes. 2. Matching upper bound (Theorem 3.2): L = O �min(k,⌈k/s⌉·⌈log2(2s)⌉)·⌈log2 n/(mp)⌉  via win- dowed pointer doubling. 3. Bandwidth barrier (Theorem 3.6): Any per-window distinguishability-based argument cannot prove L = ω(⌈k/s⌉) when Hmp ≥log2 n. 2 4. Three-part error analysis under random caches (Theorems 3.8, 3.9, Corollary 3.10): Under oblivious caches, Pr[all T stages succeed] ≤(s/(n−T))T +2T 3/n—exponential decay in T. Under adaptive locality-respecting caches, the same probability is Θ(s/n), tight with an explicit construction. This gives an Ω((n/s)T−1) separation between adaptive and oblivious, explaining why heavy-hitter eviction dominates random eviction empirically. Relation to prior work. Sanford et al. (2024) proved O(logk) depth suffices for k-hop pointer chasing with full cache (s = n), and Ω(logk) is necessary under an MPC conjecture. Their analysis does not address cache constraints and assumes infinite precision. The two results are complementary: theirs is conditional and cache-oblivious; ours is unconditional (modulo locality) but finite-precision. In the s = n limit, our bound gives L ≥⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉, which for Hmp = O(logn) matches Ω(logn) ≥Ω(logk); in larger Hmp, our bound is weaker and cannot recover their Ω(logk), reflecting the bandwidth barrier. Peng et al. (2024) use communication complexity to prove a related inability of shallow Transformers to compose functions (e.g., grandparent lookup on a genealogy), giving unconditional impossibility for low-depth models on pointer-chasing-like tasks; their work focuses on depth vs. domain size rather than depth vs. cache size, and does not address KV compression. Haris and Onak (2025) prove a Θ(nd) space lower bound (for d = Ω(logn); in the low-dimensional regime d = o(logn) they show Ω(d ·ed)) for KV cache compression in attention-based autoregressive Transformers via a reduction from communication complexity, using a Johnson-Lindenstrauss-style construction; this is orthogonal to our depth-vs-cache tradeoff, focusing on whether sublinear space suffices at all rather than on the interaction between depth and cache size. The concurrent empirical study (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2026) independently observed phase-transition-like behavior in multi-hop accuracy under compression. 2 Setup Definition 2.1 (Cache-Restricted Transformer). An (L,H,m, p,s)-Cache-Transformer operates on N tokens under p-bit arithmetic: every weight entry, every intermediate activation entering a subsequent layer’s input (queries, keys, values, attention-output vectors, and token representations) is drawn from a finite set Qp of cardinality at most 2p per coordinate. The softmax attention weights themselves are computed in continuous arithmetic and are not required to be p-bit quantized; only the attention outputs (the m-dimensional vectors fed into the residual connection) are rounded to Qm p before the next layer. Representations X(ℓ) i ∈Qm p are indexed by layer ℓ∈{0,...,L} and token i ∈[N]. At each layer ℓ≥1, attention is restricted to a shared subset S(ℓ) ⊆[N] with |S(ℓ)| ≤s. Tokens outside S(ℓ) are strictly inaccessible at layer ℓ: their key–value pairs are deleted and contribute exactly zero to attention. The subset S(ℓ) is chosen by a deterministic function Cℓof all layer-(ℓ−1) representations. Element-wise MLPs between layers are unrestricted in computation but their outputs are quantized to Qp coordinatewise before the next layer. For token positions j that do not participate in attention at layer ℓ(i.e., the query at position j attends to no keys, equivalently the attention output at position j is zero), we set X(ℓ) j = X(ℓ−1) j (representation carried forward unchanged via the residual connection); this convention is standard and captures the behavior of evicted-token slots in KV-compression systems. This induces a finite state space: each m-dimensional attention-output vector takes one of at most 2mp values. Remark 2.2 (Modeling assumptions). The finite-precision assumption reflects hardware reality: FP16, BF16, INT8 are standard, with p = 8–16. The counting argument (Lemma 4.2) requires only that each coordinate lies in a finite set of size ≤2p; floating-point formats such as BF16 satisfy this with p = 16, regardless of exponent/mantissa split. The hard cache boundary models physical KV cache eviction, where deleted 3 tokens’ keys and values are removed from GPU memory. Our bounds do not apply to soft compression (value quantization, token merging) or to infinite-precision theoretical models. Remark 2.3 (Per-head cache budgets). Definition 2.1 assumes a shared cache S(ℓ) across all heads. If instead each head h has its own cache S(ℓ) h with |S(ℓ) h | ≤sh, the query aggregates attention outputs across all heads, so the set of tokens that can influence it in one layer is the union S h S(ℓ) h , of size at most ∑h sh. Redistributing a fixed total budget ∑h sh across heads does not alter the bounds; only the total capacity matters. Definition 2.4 (k-hop pointer chasing, PCn,k). Input: a permutation π : [n] →[n] (where [n] = {1,2,...,n}), encoded as n input tokens at positions 1,...,n (token at position i holds the pair (i,π(i))), plus a query token at a separate position indexed 0 (so the total number of token positions is n+1, indexed {0,1,...,n}; the query position 0 is not in π’s domain). The chain is defined by z0 := 1 and zt+1 := π(zt) for t ≥0, so zt ∈[n] for all t. Output: zk = πk(1). Unless stated, we assume n ≥4k. 3 Main Results We state the four main theorems; proofs appear in Section 4. 3.1 Product lower bound (conjectured) Conjecture 3.1 (Product depth lower bound). There exist universal constants c > 0 and n0 ≥1 such that for all n ≥n0, any (L,H,m, p,s)-Cache-Transformer with a locality-respecting cache controller (Definition 4.6) solving PCn,k correctly on all inputs with n ≥4k and s ≤√n/4 satisfies L ≥c· k s  · log2 n Hmp  . (1) For s > √n/4, we conjecture only the unconditional max-bound of Proposition 4.4; whether the product structure extends to this regime is open (see Open Problem 2 in Section 8). Note that in this regime, the cache-reachability factor from Proposition 4.4 is ⌈(k −1)/s⌉rather than ⌈k/s⌉, a unit-level difference; the strengthening to ⌈k/s⌉in Lemma 4.16 uses the cycle-structure restriction and therefore applies only in the s ≤√n/4 regime. We refer to this as Conjecture 3.1 throughout; all downstream corollaries that rely on the product bound are flagged explicitly as conditional on it. Evidence for Conjecture 3.1. We provide three independent pieces of partial evidence: 1. Matching upper bound within O(H logs) (Theorem 3.2): windowed pointer doubling achieves L = O(⌈k/s⌉·⌈log2(2s)⌉·⌈log2 n/(mp)⌉). The lower bound in Conjecture 3.1 matches this upper bound up to a multiplicative factor of O(H logs), which vanishes at s = O(1) and H = 1. 2. Consistency with all known algorithms: every correct algorithm we have analyzed satisfies the conjectured bound—either via an exit-based argument (Section 4) or via an intrinsic cost argument (serial chain- tracking has L = Θ(klogn/(mp)) ≥⌈k/s⌉·⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉since sH ≥1). 3. Partial unconditional lower bound: we prove in Section 4 the max-bound L ≥max(⌈k/s⌉,⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉) unconditionally (Proposition 4.4), and the conjecture additionally holds unconditionally in the restricted subregime s·2mp < 8 under the stronger query-only controller assumption (Remark 4.13, “Partial progress” paragraph). The gap between the unconditional max-bound and the conjectured product-bound is exactly one multiplicative factor of ⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉or of ⌈k/s⌉, whichever is smaller. 4 Closing Conjecture 3.1—either by direct proof or by disproof and replacement—is the central technical question left open by this work; we discuss it as Open Problem 1 in Section 8. This conjectured bound is strictly stronger than the unconditional L ≥max(⌈k/s⌉,⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉): cache and bandwidth bottlenecks would interact multiplicatively through the sequential dependency of the pointer chain, rather than competing. The locality restriction is essential for the bandwidth factor; without it, only the cache-reachability factor ⌈k/s⌉can be proved (Lemma 4.1). Theorem 3.2 (Matching upper bound). For any n,k,s with 1 ≤s ≤n, there exists a single-head (L,1,m, p,s)- Cache-Transformer solving PCn,k with L = O �min �k, ⌈k/s⌉·⌈log2(2s)⌉  ·⌈log2 n/(mp)⌉  . (We use ⌈log2(2s)⌉≥1 to ensure the bound is non-vacuous at s = 1, where the min degenerates to the serial cost O(k ·⌈log2 n/(mp)⌉).) The two bounds combine to k s · logn Hmp ≲L ≲k s · logs · logn mp , a multiplicative gap of O(H logs), which vanishes at s = O(1) and H = 1. Corollary 3.3 (Minimum cache formula, conditional on Conjecture 3.1). Conditional on Conjecture 3.1 and in its regime (s ≤√n/4, n ≥4k), to achieve depth L with a locality-respecting controller, s = Ω  max  k L, klog2 n L·Hmp  . The first term (cache reachability) dominates when Hmp ≥log2 n; the second (bandwidth) dominates in narrow or low-precision models and is conditional on the product bound of Conjecture 3.1. Unconditionally (from Proposition 4.4), only the weaker s = Ω(k/L) is guaranteed. Corollary 3.4 (Depth transition at critical cache size). Define s⋆= n⌈log2 k⌉/k. Assume k →∞with k = o(n/logn) (which ensures both s⋆= ω(logk) and s⋆= o(n), so s⋆is a non-trivial threshold in (logk,n)) and mp = Ω(logn) (a realistic LLM precision regime). Then up to logarithmic factors: • For s ≥s⋆: windowed pointer doubling achieves L = O((k/s)logs·logn/(mp)), and the ratio (L/k) = O((logslogn)/(s·mp)) = o(1) under the precision assumption, so L = o(k). • For s < s⋆: any correct algorithm requires L ≥⌈k/s⌉; the serial strategy achieves L = O(klogn/(mp)) = O(k). Below s⋆, the upper-bound strategy we know (serial chain-tracking) incurs L = Θ(klogn/(mp)) = Θ(k), while the (unconditional) reachability lower bound L ≥⌈k/s⌉leaves a gap: the upper-bound parallelism advantage over RNNs disappears in the known algorithm, but the lower bound at s ≫1 does not rule out faster parallel algorithms in principle. For s = O(1) specifically, ⌈k/s⌉= Θ(k) matches the serial upper bound, giving a tight Θ(k) characterization in that sub-regime—comparable to RNNs (which are stuck at Θ(k) under (Sanford et al., 2024)’s conditional lower bound requiring RNN width O(N/k6)). 3.2 When the product bound binds: the bandwidth barrier For standard LLM configurations (H = 32, m = 128, p = 16, so Hmp ≈6.5 · 104), the bandwidth factor ⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉equals 1 for any realistic n (up to n ≤2Hmp ≫1019000), and Conjecture 3.1 reduces to the cache-reachability bound L ≥⌈k/s⌉alone—a near-trivial consequence of the fact that the query token’s reachable set grows by at most s per layer. It is natural to ask whether the bandwidth factor can be strengthened. We show that, within a natural class of proof techniques, it cannot: any lower bound obtainable via per-window distinguishability counting saturates at ⌈k/s⌉when Hmp ≥log2 n. 5 Definition 3.5 (Per-window distinguishability counting argument). A lower-bound proof is a per-window distinguishability counting argument if it decomposes L ≥W · B, where W = Ω(⌈k/s⌉) is a number of windows and B is a per-window depth bound of the form B ≥ log2 Mj Hmp  , where Mj is a lower bound, at window j, on the number of distinct per-window outputs zτ j that the algorithm must distinguish among inputs sharing a common pre-window query-state X(ℓj−1) 0 . This class includes the proof strategy for Conjecture 3.1, all reachability-plus-counting combinations, and finite-precision adaptations of (Sanford et al., 2024)’s technique. Theorem 3.6 (Bandwidth barrier). Let A be any per-window distinguishability counting argument (Defini- tion 3.5) applied to PCn,k. Then for each window j, the per-window output count Mj satisfies Mj ≤n, so the bandwidth factor B derived from A satisfies B ≤⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉. In particular, when Hmp ≥log2 n, B ≤1, and A yields at most L ≥W = O(⌈k/s⌉), matching the cache-reachability bound. Furthermore, the barrier is structural: any modification of PCn,k that keeps per-window output within [n] inherits Mj ≤n and the same saturation. Remark 3.7 (On the conceptual depth of the barrier). In some sense, Theorem 3.6 is a direct consequence of the fact that the per-window output lies in [n], making it look tautological. However, its role is to formalize a class of proof techniques (Definition 3.5) and show that the entire class ceilings at ⌈k/s⌉when Hmp ≥log2 n. This is analogous to formalizing “natural proofs” in circuit complexity: once the class is defined, the impossibility within that class is near-immediate, but the formalization itself identifies what bypass mechanisms are needed. Our “eight handles” analysis (Section 4.4) systematically confirms that no bypass we could identify escapes the class. Proof. The per-window output of PCn,k is zτj = πτj(1) ∈[n], which takes at most n distinct values as π varies (regardless of any conditioning). By the definition of Mj (Definition 3.5) as a lower bound on the support size of zτj under some conditioning, we have Mj ≤n. The inequality B ≤⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉is then immediate, and equals 1 when Hmp ≥log2 n. The modification claim is immediate: any task with per-window output in a set of size ≤n gives Mj ≤n. We discuss in Section 8 what it would take to break this barrier: translating unconditional communication- complexity lower bounds for pointer chasing (Nisan and Wigderson, 1993; Yehudayoff, 2020; Mao et al., 2025) into Cache-Transformer depth lower bounds without the MPC conjecture (an open problem) or genuinely different proof techniques not of the form in Definition 3.5. The barrier is not a proof gap in our work; it is a structural obstacle on the current road to depth lower bounds in the standard LLM regime. 3.3 Error amplification under random cache selection The empirical observation (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2026) that multi-hop accuracy drops precipitously under random cache selection can be partially explained by bounds on the joint success probability of windowed pointer doubling. We analyze this algorithm: at each of T = ⌈log2 k⌉stages, the algorithm needs to find a destination dt = πt(1) (for simplicity of exposition we take a consecutive-destination chain; the pointer-doubling structure is analogous) in a cache Ct ⊆[n] of size s. The algorithm succeeds at stage t if dt ∈Ct, and succeeds overall (event E ) if all T stages succeed. We establish three results: a tight exponential bound for oblivious caches, a tight stage-1-dominated bound for adaptive caches matched by an explicit construction, and a quantitative separation between the two. 6 Theorem 3.8 (Oblivious caches: exponential decay in T). Let π ∼Unif(Sn) with n ≥T +1. For any oblivious cache selection (each Ct chosen without access to π; the Ct may share common randomness), Pr[E ] ≤  s n−T T + 2T 3 n . In the sub-regime where the main term dominates the error term—precisely when sT ≥2T 3nT−1 (equivalently (s/n)T ≥2T 3/n)—the bound exhibits exponential decay in T of rate (s/n)T. Outside this sub-regime (e.g., s = o(n) with T = ω(1) and sT growing slower than T 3nT−1) the error term 2T 3/n dominates and the bound degenerates to O(T 3/n). Theorem 3.9 (Adaptive caches: tight Θ(s/n)). Let π ∼Unif(Sn) with n ≥T +1. For any locality-respecting adaptive cache controller (Definition 4.6), Pr[E ] ≤s n. Moreover, this is tight: there is an explicit locality-respecting adaptive controller (“chain-tracking”) achieving Pr[E ] ≥s/n, matching the upper bound with exact equality. Corollary 3.10 (Adaptive-oblivious separation). In the regime sT ≥2T 3nT−1 (equivalently, s ≥(2T 3)1/T · n(T−1)/T) and T ≤√n/2, in which the main term of Theorem 3.8 dominates the error term, the optimal adaptive success probability exceeds the best oblivious success probability by a factor of at least Pr[E ]opt adaptive Pr[E ]opt oblivious ≥Ω n s T−1 . Outside this regime, the separation is Ω(s/T 3), still a non-trivial multiplicative gap. Remark 3.11 (What this says about adaptive vs. oblivious eviction). Theorems 3.8 and 3.9 together give a sharp answer to why adaptive (heavy-hitter, attention-score) eviction outperforms oblivious (recency, random) baselines in practice, as reported by Liu et al. (2025): the two regimes exhibit qualitatively different scaling behavior with chain length. Under uniform random cache selection—the worst case for the algorithm— oblivious caches see joint success probability decay as (s/n)T, while adaptive caches see decay as only s/n (no T dependence). The adaptive controller exploits the structure that learning early chain values reveals later ones via locality. Our bound is non-asymptotic and parameter-free: it applies to any locality-respecting controller, and the achievability construction demonstrates the bound cannot be improved beyond constants. Remark 3.12 (Relation to empirical observations). At n = 16,s = 8,k = 8 (.5%. The main term of Theorem 3.8 gives (s/(n−T))T = (8/13)3 ≈23.3%, which consistently upper-bounds the good-chain success probability Pr[E good]; however, Theorem 3.8’s full bound (s/(n−T))T +2T 3/n ≈361% is vacuous (exceeds 1) at this small n due to the error term. We distinguish two senses of “non-vacuous”: (a) bound < 1, requiring only 2T 3/n < 1, i.e., n > 2T 3 (n ≥55 for T = 3); (b) main term dominates error term, i.e., (s/n)T > 2T 3/n, equivalently sT/nT−1 > 2T 3, i.e., n < sT/(T−1)/(2T 3)1/(T−1) [equivalently n > 2T 3/(s/n)T in the fixed-ratio parameterization]. For T = 3 and s/n = c constant, this gives n > 2T 3/cT = 54/c3; e.g., c = 1/2 requires n > 432, while c ≈1 (near-full cache) gives n ≳54. In sense (a) the theorem rules out 100% success; in sense (b) the theorem’s (s/n)T decay rate is the informative feature. The main term alone empirically upper-bounds good-chain success for all n ≥T +1. Quantitative sharpness of the full bound requires moderate-to-large n. When the bandwidth factor binds quantitatively. In the barrier regime (Hmp ≥log2 n), the product bound of Conjecture 3.1 would reduce to ⌈k/s⌉and coincide with the cache-reachability bound. In the narrow-model regime (Hmp < log2 n: e.g., H = 1, m = 4, p = 4 giving Hmp = 16; or single-head INT4 with m = 16, p = 4, H = 1), the bandwidth factor is strictly greater than 1 and the conjectured product structure is quantitatively binding. This regime is increasingly relevant for quantized and edge-deployed models. 7 4 Proofs 4.1 Cache reachability The first ingredient is a size bound on the set of input tokens on which the query-token representation can functionally depend. This set grows by at most s per layer, reflecting the fact that the cache at each layer adds s new indices to the query’s reachable support. Lemma 4.1 (Reachability). Define the functional-dependence set Dℓ(0)(π) ⊆[N] of the query token at layer ℓ, for each input π, as the minimal set of input tokens such that X(ℓ) 0 (π) is a function of {xj(π) : j ∈Dℓ(0)(π)} together with the (deterministic) weights and cache-selection rules. The set is defined pointwise in π (not as a single fixed set covering all π), which is the right notion for adaptive controllers where the reachable support varies with the input. Then D0(0) = {0}, Dℓ(0) ⊆Dℓ−1(0)∪S(ℓ) ∪ [ j∈S(ℓ) Dℓ−1(j), and in particular |DL(0)| ≤1+L·s. (2) Proof. The first inclusion is immediate from the attention update rule: X(ℓ) 0 is computed from X(ℓ−1) 0 and the keys/values of tokens j ∈S(ℓ), which in turn are functions of X(ℓ−1) j . So X(ℓ) 0 depends only on layer-(ℓ−1) representations of tokens in {0}∪S(ℓ), each of which depends only on inputs in Dℓ−1(j). For the size bound (2), the naive induction on the recursion above does not work: S j∈S(ℓ) Dℓ−1(j) can be as large as |S(ℓ)|·maxj |Dℓ−1(j)|. Instead, we argue differently. For each layer ℓ′ ∈{1,...,L}, let Nℓ′ = S(ℓ′) be the set of cache-accessible token indices at that layer. We claim DL(0) ⊆{0}∪ L[ ℓ′=1 Nℓ′. (3) Given (3), the size bound follows immediately: |DL(0)| ≤1+∑L ℓ′=1 |S(ℓ′)| ≤1+Ls. To prove (3), we show by induction on ℓthat for every token i ∈[N], Dℓ(i) ⊆{i}∪ ℓ[ ℓ′=1 Nℓ′. (4) Base case ℓ= 0: D0(i) = {i}, trivially contained in the right-hand side. Inductive step: by the recursion, Dℓ(i) ⊆Dℓ−1(i)∪S(ℓ) ∪S j∈S(ℓ) Dℓ−1(j). By the inductive hypothesis applied to i and to each j ∈S(ℓ) = Nℓ, Dℓ−1(i) ⊆{i}∪ ℓ−1 [ ℓ′=1 Nℓ′, Dℓ−1(j) ⊆{j}∪ ℓ−1 [ ℓ′=1 Nℓ′. Taking the union with Nℓand using { j} ⊆Nℓfor j ∈Nℓ: Dℓ(i) ⊆{i}∪Nℓ∪ ℓ−1 [ ℓ′=1 Nℓ′ = {i}∪ ℓ[ ℓ′=1 Nℓ′. Observe that Lemma 4.1 does not depend on how the caches S(ℓ) are chosen—adaptive or oblivious, data-dependent or not—because the cache sets enter the bound as sets of indices, and the union argument is index-based. This is crucial for the trace-equivalence argument (Lemma 4.10) below. 8 4.2 Bandwidth bound via counting Lemma 4.2 (Distinguishability bound). Fix any layer ℓ0 and any value x ∈Qm p . Over all inputs π such that X(ℓ0) 0 (π) = x, and over all adaptive cache choices, the set ΣB(x) :=   X(ℓ0+B) 0 (π) : π such that X(ℓ0) 0 (π) = x has cardinality at most 2B·Hmp. Proof. At each layer ℓ, the query-token update takes the form X(ℓ) 0 = φℓ X(ℓ−1) 0 + H ∑ h=1 a(ℓ,h) 0 W (ℓ,h) O ! , where φℓis the deterministic MLP+quantization map and a(ℓ,h) 0 ∈Qm p is the quantized attention output of head h. By Definition 2.1, every intermediate activation—including each a(ℓ,h) 0 —is quantized to p bits per coordinate before use, so |Qm p | ≤2mp. The softmax attention weights may themselves be computed in continuous arithmetic, but the attention output vectors a(ℓ,h) 0 are rounded to Qm p before being added via the residual connection; our bound applies to these outputs, not to the internal softmax computation. Thus, given a fixed X(ℓ−1) 0 , the next-layer value X(ℓ) 0 is determined by the tuple (a(ℓ,1) 0 ,...,a(ℓ,H) 0 ) ∈(Qm p )H, of which there are at most (2mp)H = 2Hmp. Iterating from X(ℓ0) 0 = x for B layers gives |ΣB(x)| ≤(2Hmp)B = 2B·Hmp. (Note that the trivial bound |ΣB(x)| ≤|Qm p | = 2mp also holds for all B ≥1, since ΣB(x) ⊆Qm p . The B-dependent form 2B·Hmp is looser in absolute terms when BH ≥1, but it is the form required downstream: we use this lemma to prove a lower bound on B, so expressing the reachable-state count as growing with B is essential—the trivial 2mp bound is independent of B and cannot force B to be large.) Remark 4.3 (Why this avoids the softmax objection). We make no claim about how softmax computes attention weights. Those weights can be continuous, data-dependent, and arbitrarily complex. Our bound applies to the output of attention—an m-dimensional vector quantized to p bits—not to the internal mechanism. This is a physical constraint: GPU hardware represents attention outputs in finite precision. 4.3 Sequential windows Before presenting the full proof strategy, we record an unconditional lower bound that follows from Lem- mas 4.1 and 4.2 alone (no invocation of Lemma 4.11 or Lemma 4.14): Proposition 4.4 (Unconditional max-bound). Any (L,H,m, p,s)-Cache-Transformer solving PCn,k correctly on all inputs with n ≥4k satisfies L ≥max k −1 s  , log2 n Hmp  ≥max  ⌈k/s⌉−1, log2 n Hmp  . Proof. Cache-reachability factor. Fix any π with cycle of 1 of length > k (such π exist for n ≥k +1), so that z0 = 1,z1,...,zk are pairwise distinct. For the algorithm to output zk(π) = π(zk−1(π)) correctly, its final query-token state X(L) 0 must depend on the value π(zk−1). By Lemma 4.1 (applied at layer L), X(L) 0 is determined by π only through π|TL(π); in particular, dependence on π(zk−1) requires zk−1 ∈TL(π). Iterating the same argument for zk−2,zk−3,... (since knowing zk−1 = π(zk−2) likewise requires zk−2 ∈TL, and so on), we obtain {z0,z1,...,zk−1} ⊆TL(π). These k positions are distinct, so |TL(π)| ≥k. Combined with Lemma 4.1’s upper bound |TL(π)| ≤1+Ls, we get 1+Ls ≥k, i.e., L ≥⌈(k −1)/s⌉. 9 Output-bandwidth factor. A correct algorithm determines zk from the final query-token state X(L) 0 , so the map π 7→X(L) 0 (π) must take at least n distinct values: for each target value v ∈[n], there exists a permutation πv ∈Sn with zk(πv) = v. For v ̸= 1: pick any k +1 distinct elements 1 = a0,a1,...,ak−1,ak = v of [n] (valid since n ≥4k ≥k+1); set πv(ai) = ai+1 for i = 0,...,k−1, and extend πv arbitrarily to a bijection on [n]. Then zi(πv) = ai for i = 0,...,k, so zk = v. For v = 1: take πv with a cycle of length exactly k on {1,a1,...,ak−1} (for any distinct a1,...,ak−1 ∈[n]\{1}), i.e., πv(1) = a1,πv(ai) = ai+1 for i = 1,...,k−2, and πv(ak−1) = 1; then zi(πv) = ai for i = 1,...,k−1 and zk(πv) = 1 = v. Distinct v’s require distinct final states. By Lemma 4.2 applied from the fixed initial state X(0) 0 , |ΣL(X(0) 0 )| ≤2L·Hmp, hence 2LHmp ≥n, i.e., L ≥⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉. Conjecture 3.1 would strengthen this max to a product. The proof strategy occupying the rest of this section (Lemmas 4.11–4.19) implements the exit-based argument outlined in Section 3; the probabilistic step (⋆) of Lemma 4.11 is left as Open Problem 1. The ⌈(k −1)/s⌉cache-reachability factor of Proposition 4.4 is strengthened to ⌈k/s⌉via the sequential-windows argument (Lemma 4.16); since ⌈(k −1)/s⌉≥⌈k/s⌉−1, this is a unit-level refinement and does not affect asymptotics. Definition 4.5. Partition the chain into W = ⌈k/s⌉windows. Window j ∈{1,...,W} covers chain positions z(j−1)s,...,zmin(js,k); its boundary output is zmin(js,k). For brevity write τ j := min(js,k), so window j’s output is zτj. Handling adaptive cache: trace equivalence under a locality assumption A subtle issue is that the cache controller Cℓis defined (Definition 2.1) as a deterministic function of all layer-(ℓ−1) representations, not just those in Dℓ−1(0). A maximally adversarial controller could in principle read representations of tokens it has not yet cached, and thereby leak information about out-of-cache tokens into its subsequent cache choices. We rule this out with a natural restriction: Definition 4.6 (Locality-respecting controller). A cache controller Cℓis locality-respecting if, for every π, the set S(ℓ)(π) depends only on the representations {X(ℓ−1) j (π) : j ∈Tℓ(π)}, where Tℓ(π) := {0}∪S ℓ′<ℓS(ℓ′)(π) is the cache support up to layer ℓ−1. (This recursive definition is well-defined, as shown in Proposition 4.8 below.) Remark 4.7 (Relation to Definition 2.1). Definition 2.1 (model) permits Cℓto depend on all N representations at layer ℓ−1; Definition 4.6 (locality) further restricts this to positions the algorithm has already touched. Our lower bounds (Proposition 4.4, Conjecture 3.1, Theorem 3.6) apply to the locality-respecting class (a strict subclass); our upper bound (Theorem 3.2) is realized by a locality-respecting algorithm and hence applies to both classes. For positions j ∈Tℓ\S(ℓ−1), the representation X(ℓ−1) j is carried forward from the last layer at which j participated in attention (residually unchanged); these are the “ghost states” for evicted tokens, consistent with KV compression systems in practice. Proposition 4.8 (Well-definedness of the recursion). The recursive definition is well-defined: for each ℓ∈{0,1,...,L}, Tℓ(π), S(ℓ)(π), and all representations X(ℓ) j (π) are deterministic functions of π|Tℓ(π). Proof. By strong induction on ℓ. For ℓ= 0: T0(π) = {0} (constant, not a function of π at all); X(0) j (π) = xj(π) is the identity embedding, depending on π only at position j, so X(0) j for j ∈T0 depends on π|T0. For ℓ≥1: assume the proposition holds for all ℓ′ < ℓ. Then Tℓ−1(π) = {0}∪S ℓ′<ℓ−1 S(ℓ′)(π) is a function of π|Tℓ−2(π) by the inductive hypothesis applied to ℓ−1, and S(ℓ−1)(π) is a function of {X(ℓ−2) j (π) : j ∈ Tℓ−1(π)} by locality. Hence Tℓ(π) = Tℓ−1(π)∪S(ℓ−1)(π) is a function of π|Tℓ−1(π). 10 Representations X(ℓ) j for j ∈Tℓare computed by attention over S(ℓ)(π) ⊆Tℓ(π) (by definition of Tℓ), using as keys/values the layer-(ℓ−1) representations at positions in S(ℓ). By the inductive hypothesis, these representations are functions of π|Tℓ−1 ⊆π|Tℓ, completing the induction. Under this restriction, the controller at layer ℓonly examines representations of tokens it has already accessed at some earlier layer. This restriction is satisfied by essentially all KV-compression methods (H2O, SnapKV, StreamingLLM, PyramidKV, heavy-hitter variants), and is physically natural: reading representations outside the cache would require loading them, defeating the purpose of cache compression. We briefly discuss the fully adversarial case in Open Problem 3 in the Discussion section. The lower bounds below implicitly assume a locality-respecting controller; this is made explicit in Conjecture 3.1. Definition 4.9 (Cache trace). For a fixed algorithm and a fixed input π, the cache trace is T (π) := (S(1)(π),...,S(L)(π)), and its support is T(π) := {0}∪SL ℓ=1 S(ℓ)(π). By Lemma 4.1, |T(π)| ≤1+Ls. Lemma 4.10 (Trace equivalence). Fix any algorithm with a locality-respecting controller. Suppose π1,π2 satisfy π1|T(π1) = π2|T(π1). Then T (π1) = T (π2), T(π1) = T(π2), and X(ℓ) j (π1) = X(ℓ) j (π2) for all ℓ∈ {0,...,L} and j ∈T(π1). In particular, X(L) 0 (π1) = X(L) 0 (π2). Proof. Write T1 := T(π1). We prove by induction on ℓ∈{0,1,...,L} the joint claim (Hℓ): S(ℓ′)(π2) = S(ℓ′)(π1) for all ℓ′ ≤ℓ, and X(ℓ′) j (π2) = X(ℓ′) j (π1) for all ℓ′ ≤ℓand j ∈T1. Base case ℓ= 0. The representation claim holds by hypothesis and because X(0) j is a fixed function of x j. The cache claim is vacuous at ℓ′ = 0. Inductive step ℓ−1 ⇒ℓ. Assume (Hℓ−1). Step 1: Cache selection matches. By locality, S(ℓ)(π) depends only on {X(ℓ−1) j (π) : j ∈Tℓ(π)}. By (Hℓ−1), S(ℓ′)(π2) = S(ℓ′)(π1) for ℓ′ < ℓ, so Tℓ(π2) = Tℓ(π1) =: T ⋆ ℓ⊆T1. By (Hℓ−1), X(ℓ−1) j (π2) = X(ℓ−1) j (π1) for j ∈T ⋆ ℓ. So the controller’s input tuple is identical under π1 and π2, hence S(ℓ)(π2) = S(ℓ)(π1). Step 2: Representations on T1 match. With S(ℓ)(π1) = S(ℓ)(π2) =: S(ℓ) ⊆T1, for any j ∈T1 the update X(ℓ) j (π) = fℓ �X(ℓ−1) j (π), {(X(ℓ−1) i (π),Ki(π),Vi(π)) : i ∈S(ℓ)}  receives inputs X(ℓ−1) j (with j ∈T1, equal by (Hℓ−1)) and X(ℓ−1) i for i ∈S(ℓ) ⊆T1 (also equal). Hence X(ℓ) j (π2) = X(ℓ) j (π1). This closes the induction. Since 0 ∈T1, X(L) 0 (π1) = X(L) 0 (π2). The locality assumption is exactly what lets Step 1 close: under a general controller, reading X(ℓ−1) j for some j /∈T1 could distinguish π1 from π2 and cause S(ℓ) to diverge. Lemma 4.11 (Existence of a chain-exiting permutation). Fix any algorithm with a locality-respecting controller. Suppose L ≤n/(8s), n ≥4k, and τj ≥2 (which excludes only the corner case τ j = 1, i.e., j = 1 together with min(s,k) = 1). Then there exists a permutation π1 and an index t⋆∈(τj−1,τ j] such that zt⋆−1(π1) /∈T1 := T(π1). Proof sketch of Lemma 4.11. By Lemma 4.1 and the hypothesis L ≤n/(8s), |T(π)| ≤1+Ls ≤n/4 for every π, whenever n ≥8. We sketch a probabilistic argument; see Remark 4.13 for a discussion of the remaining technical step. Under the assumption τ j ≥2, let σ j := τj −1 ≥1 (we use σj throughout this proof and Remark 4.13 to avoid confusion with the window-boundary index τj−1, which in general differs from τj −1 when s ≥2). So 11 zσj is well-defined and, by distinctness (below), is not the fixed starting position z0 = 1 (which is always in T for a correct algorithm; see the proof of Lemma 4.14, Part (c)). Average over π ∼Unif(Sn) and consider Pr[zσ j(π) ∈T(π)]. Two facts: • Distinctness. Let Dchain be the event that z0,...,zk are pairwise distinct, equivalently, the cycle of π containing 1 has length > k. For π ∼Unif(Sn), the cycle length of 1 is uniform on {1,...,n}, so Pr[Dchain] = 1−k/n ≥3/4. • Marginal of endpoint. Conditional on Dchain and on σj ≥1, by symmetry within the cycle, zσ j(π) is uniform on [n]\{1}. The desired conclusion Pr[zσ j ∈T] < 1 would follow from: (⋆) Pr[zσ j(π) ∈T(π) | Dchain] ≤E[|T(π)|] n−1 ≤ n/4 n−1 < 1/3, giving Pr[zσ j ∈T] ≤1/3+1/4 = 7/12 < 1. Existence of some π1 with zσj(π1) /∈T(π1) then follows, and taking t⋆≤τ j to be the first chain position outside T(π1) proves the lemma. Remark 4.12 (Corner case τ j = 1). When τj = 1—that is, j = 1 together with min(s,k) = 1 (so either s = 1 or k = 1)—Lemma 4.11 is vacuously false: the only candidate t⋆= 1 requires z0 = 1 /∈T(π), which fails for any correct algorithm because 1 ∈TL is forced by reading π(1) (proof of Lemma 4.14, Part (c)). However, this does not affect the proof strategy for Conjecture 3.1: we can directly establish a per-window cost ℓ1 −ℓ0 ≥B−1 for window 1 (with ℓ0 := 0) via the bandwidth argument alone. Using the definition of ℓj given in the proof strategy below (ℓj is the earliest layer at which |Tℓ| ≥τj, see Section 4.3), window 1’s cache support must grow to |Tℓ1| ≥1 = τ1, which holds already at ℓ1 = 0 since T0 = {0} has size 1; but to additionally determine z1 = π(1) ∈[n] requires, by Lemma 4.2 applied from X(0) 0 , that X(ℓ⋆ 1) 0 at some layer ℓ⋆ 1 ≤L takes at least n distinct values, giving ℓ⋆ 1 ≥⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉= B. Such ℓ⋆ 1 exists and satisfies ℓ⋆ 1 ≤L: the final state X(L) 0 must determine zk, and for k ≥1 distinguishing any two π,π′ with zk(π) ̸= zk(π′) requires X(L) 0 (π) ̸= X(L) 0 (π′), so X(L) 0 takes at least n distinct values (all n outputs are realized; see Proposition 4.4’s output-bandwidth argument for the explicit construction). Hence L ≥ℓ⋆ 1 ≥B. For k = 1: W = 1 and this already establishes the product bound L ≥B = ⌈k/s⌉·B (trivially, since ⌈1/s⌉= 1). For s = 1,k ≥2: combine with the j ≥2 windows handled by Lemma 4.11 to obtain the full W ·B product bound. Remark 4.13 (A technical gap in Lemma 4.11). The proof sketch above reduces Lemma 4.11 to inequality (⋆). Establishing (⋆) requires controlling the joint distribution of (T(π),zσ j(π)) conditional on Dchain, where σ j := τj −1. Both quantities are π-measurable, and for an adaptive, label-sensitive algorithm they are not independent in general: the algorithm’s cache trajectory could in principle correlate with specific chain values. A naive union bound yields only Pr[zσj ∈T|D] ≤E[|T|]/Pr[D] ≤n/3, which is too weak; tightening to (⋆) appears to require either a second-moment argument specific to the input distribution or a Yao-principle reduction against an explicit adversary. Status of Conjecture 3.1. Lemmas 4.1, 4.2, 4.14, 4.16, 4.19 are proved without hidden probabilistic assumptions. Lemma 4.17 (Window incompressibility) depends on Lemma 4.11 (it invokes the existence of a window- j exit) and therefore inherits the gap. The proof strategy of Section 4.3 is therefore complete modulo (⋆): establishing (⋆)—either by proving it for the uniform distribution or by exhibiting an adversarial family on which the conclusion of Lemma 4.11 holds directly—would turn Conjecture 3.1 into a theorem. Closing the gap is stated as Open Problem 1 in Section 8. Partial progress: unconditional cache-coverage bound in a restricted parameter subregime, under a stronger controller assumption. We can establish Lemma 4.11 unconditionally under (i) the additional parameter hypothesis s·2mp < 8 (equivalent to mp < 3−log2 s) and (ii) the stronger controller assumption that Cℓis a deterministic function of the query-token state X(ℓ−1) 0 alone (rather than of all {X(ℓ−1) j : j ∈Tℓ} as in 12 Definition 4.6). This query-only controller class is strictly narrower than locality-respecting but still includes all known KV-compression heuristics that dispatch cache selection from a single “read-head” computation. Under (ii), the cache at each layer Cℓtakes at most 2mp distinct values across possible query states, yielding at most s·2mp distinct (state, slot) pairs, hence at most s·2mp distinct positions in [n] that can ever appear in the cache at layer ℓacross different π. Summed over ℓlayers: at most ℓ·s·2mp distinct (layer, position) pairs. For chain-in-trace (zt(π) ∈T(π) for all π ∈D and all t ≤τ j −1), every chain value zt(π) for every π must be a (layer, position) pair reached. Over π, zt takes ∼n distinct values, so we need ℓ·s·2mp ≥n−1, i.e., ℓ≥(n−1)/(s·2mp). Combined with the hypothesis L ≤n/(8s) of Lemma 4.11: under (i) s·2mp < 8, we get ℓ≥(n−1)/(s·2mp) > (n−1)/8 > n/(8s) ≥L for s ≥1 and n ≥2, contradicting ℓ≤L—so exit exists. The subregime s·2mp < 8 is restrictive; it captures only very low-precision scenarios (e.g., p = 1,m = 3,s = 1, giving mp = 3). This counting argument does not extend to moderate mp (the cumulative cache coverage L · s · 2mp grows beyond n, permitting chain-in-trace without contradicting the layer budget), and it does not directly extend to general locality-respecting controllers (where Cℓcan depend on all representations at positions in Tℓ, so the cache-choice space is 2mp|Tℓ| rather than 2mp). We flag this gap explicitly here, rather than obscure it, to give the reader an accurate picture of which parts of the argument are rigorous and which depend on a yet-unverified probabilistic step. Lemma 4.14 (Adversarial permutation with preserved trace). Fix any algorithm with a locality-respecting controller. Fix a reference π1 and exit index t⋆∈(τj−1,τ j] with u := zt⋆−1(π1) /∈T1 := T(π1), as provided by Lemma 4.11. Define the target set D := [n]\ �T1 ∪{z0,...,zk}(π1)∪π1(T1)  , of size |D| ≥n−2(1+Ls)−(k +1). For each d ∈D, there is a permutation πd such that: (a) πd|T1 = π1|T1; (b) by (a) and Lemma 4.10, T (πd) = T (π1), T(πd) = T1, and X(ℓ) 0 (πd) = X(ℓ) 0 (π1) for all ℓ; (c) zt⋆(πd) = d, and distinct d,d′ ∈D yield distinct zt⋆(since zt⋆(πd) = d). Proof. Construction of πd. For d ∈D, observe π−1 1 (d) /∈T1: if π−1 1 (d) ∈T1, then d ∈π1(T1), contradicting d /∈π1(T1). Define πd(u) := d, πd(π−1 1 (d)) := π1(u), πd(x) := π1(x) for all x /∈{u,π−1 1 (d)}. This is a value-swap (transposition in the codomain) of π1, hence a permutation. Both modified positions lie in [n]\T1, so πd|T1 = π1|T1, giving (a). Part (b) follows from (a) by Lemma 4.10. Part (c). We track the chain of πd up to step t⋆. By Lemma 4.11, there exists an exit step in window j, so (taking π1 to realize this) the π1-orbit of z0 = 1 has length at least t⋆, i.e., z0,z1,...,zt⋆−1 are pairwise distinct under π1. The chain up to zt⋆−1 is the same under πd as under π1: this uses that πd agrees with π1 on positions z0,z1,...,zt⋆−2. To see this: first note z0 = 1 ∈T1. This is because a correct algorithm must compute z1 = π(1) as part of any multi-hop chain for k ≥1; by Lemma 4.1, X(L) 0 depends on π only through π|TL, so the state cannot depend on π(1) unless 1 ∈TL. Since the same argument applies at the first layer where the chain is consulted—and in particular whenever π1 has a chain-in-trace prefix of positive length—1 ∈T1. Now, πd changes π1 only at u = zt⋆−1(π1) and at π−1 1 (d); neither of these positions lies on the π1-chain prefix z0,...,zt⋆−2. For u = zt⋆−1: excluded by definition of t⋆(the first chain position outside T1; all earlier z0,...,zt⋆−2 either lie in T1 or precede u in the orbit, and u itself is the (t⋆−1)-th iterate, not an earlier one). For π−1 1 (d): we have d /∈{z1,...,zk}(π1) by construction, which (together with π1’s injectivity on the orbit) gives π−1 1 (d) /∈{z0,...,zk−1}(π1), in particular not among z0,...,zt⋆−2. Hence πd|{z0,...,zt⋆−2} = π1|{z0,...,zt⋆−2}, and the πd-chain from z0 coincides with the π1-chain up to zt⋆−1, with zt⋆(πd) = πd(zt⋆−1) = πd(u) = d by construction. 13 At step t⋆: zt⋆(πd) = πd(zt⋆−1(πd)) = πd(u) = d. For distinct d,d′ ∈D, we get distinct zt⋆values, as claimed. Remark 4.15 (Stopping at t⋆vs. reaching τj). Lemma 4.14 gives distinctness of zt⋆, not directly zτ j. When t⋆= τ j, this is what we want. When t⋆< τ j, the chain continues from d for τj −t⋆more steps before reaching zτj, and a priori two different d,d′ ∈D could produce the same zτ j through a chain collision (e.g., if d lies in a 2-cycle of π1). We avoid this in two steps, both developed in the proof of Lemma 4.17: (i) Fix the first-exit step t⋆ 0 by pigeonhole. The set N of permutations with a window-j exit partitions by first-exit step into N = F t∈(τ j−1,τj] Nt (at most s parts). By pigeonhole at least one Nt⋆ 0 has density ≥1/s within N . Restrict to π1 ∈Nt⋆ 0. (ii) Restrict further to π1 with no short non-principal cycles. For s ≤√n/4, permutations with all non- principal cycles of length > s form a positive-density subset of the conditioned ensemble. Both restrictions reduce the set of valid reference permutations we may choose, but once a single valid π1 is fixed, the clean-target counting of Lemma 4.17 gives distinct zτj values across the entire clean set of targets—no 1/s loss propagates to the distinct-value count. The O(H logs) gap between Conjecture 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 is thus attributable entirely to the upper bound’s log2(2s) factor (from windowed doubling’s inner loop), not to the lower-bound proof. Lemma 4.16 (Windows are sequential). Assume n ≥4k and s ≤√n/4. Suppose the algorithm, at layer ℓj, produces a representation X(ℓj) 0 that determines zτ j. Then ℓj ·s ≥τj = min(js,k). In particular, ℓj ≥j when js ≤k. (Outside the regime s ≤√n/4, Proposition 4.4 unconditionally gives ℓj ·s ≥τj −1; the unit-level strengthening here follows the proof strategy of Lemma 4.17.) Proof. By Lemma 4.1, X(ℓj) 0 depends only on inputs in Dℓj(0) ⊆T(π). Suppose for contradiction ℓjs < τ j. Then |T(π)| ≤1+ℓjs < 1+τj, so the chain z0,z1,...,zτj contains at least one token zt /∈T(π). Fix such a π = π1 additionally satisfying the cycle-structure restriction of Lemma 4.17 (all non-principal cycles of π1 have length > s). Such π1 exist in the conditioned ensemble with positive density for s ≤√n/4. Take the smallest t ∈{1,...,τj} with zt−1(π1) /∈T1 := T(π1). Since n ≥4k and |T1| ≤1+ℓjs ≤1+Ls, the complement D := [n]\(T1 ∪{z0,...,zk}∪π1(T1)) has size |D| ≥n−2(1+Ls)−(k +1) ≥n/2−k −O(1) in the regime L ≤n/(8s) (otherwise L ≥k/s already holds and the lemma is vacuous). Apply Lemma 4.14 to π1 and target d ∈D: it yields πd with πd|T1 = π1|T1, T (πd) = T (π1), and by part (c), zt⋆(πd) = d. Since d /∈{z0,...,zk}(π1), we have zt⋆(πd) = d ̸= zt⋆(π1). By Lemma 4.10, X(ℓj) 0 (πd) = X(ℓj) 0 (π1). We now convert this distinctness at t⋆into distinctness at τj for at least one d ∈D. When t⋆= τj: immediate. When t⋆< τj: by the clean-target counting in the proof of Lemma 4.17, under the cycle-structure restriction on π1, at most s values of d ∈D (“orbit hits u” case) fail to satisfy zτ j(πd) = πτ j−t⋆ 1 (d); the “orbit hits π−1 1 (d)” case is excluded by the cycle restriction. Since |D| ≥n/2−k −O(1) > s in the regime n ≥4k and s ≤√n/4, at least one clean d ∈D exists; for it zτj(πd) = πτj−t⋆ 1 (d) ̸= zτj(π1) (by injectivity of πτ j−t⋆ 1 together with d ̸= zt⋆(π1)). Combined with X(ℓj) 0 (πd) = X(ℓj) 0 (π1), this contradicts the assumption. Hence ℓjs ≥τj. Lemma 4.16 is only a statement about the earliest layer at which window j’s output can appear. To get the lower bound on total depth, we also need to control the number of layers that window j consumes after window j −1’s output has been resolved. This is the content of Lemma 4.17 and Lemma 4.19 below. 14 Lemma 4.17 (Window incompressibility). Assume n ≥4k and that the cache controller is locality-respecting (Definition 4.6). For any window index j ∈{1,...,W}, there exist a layer ℓ† ≤L and a value x† ∈Qm p such that, letting Π† := {π : X(ℓ†) 0 (π) = x†}: (i) for j = 1: ℓ† = 0 and x† = X(0) 0 is the fixed initial state (so Π† = Sn); (ii) for j ≥2: ℓ† = ℓj−1 (the resolution layer of window j −1). In both cases, under the additional hypothesis s ≤√n/4, zτj takes at least ⌊n/2⌋−k −O(s) distinct values as π ranges over Π†. In particular, this is ≥n/4 for n ≥n0 sufficiently large. Proof. By Lemma 4.11 (conditional on step (⋆), Remark 4.13), there exists a reference π1 with window- j chain exit. The exit index t⋆∈(τj−1,τ j] provided by Lemma 4.11 is not generally τ j, so we need an additional step to fix t⋆. By the pigeonhole argument of Remark 4.15, among the permutations N satisfying the exit property, there exists at least one exit index t⋆ 0 ∈(τj−1,τ j] such that |Nt⋆ 0|/|N | ≥1/s; we restrict attention to such π1 ∈Nt⋆ 0. We do not assume t⋆ 0 = τ j. Set ℓ† per cases (i)/(ii) and x† := X(ℓ†) 0 (π1) (in case (i), x† is the constant initial state, automatically). Apply Lemma 4.14 to this π1 with exit index t⋆= t⋆ 0. It produces a target set D of size ≥n −2(1 + Ls) −(k + 1) ≥n/2 −k −O(1). Lemma 4.14(c) gives zt⋆ 0(πd) = d with distinctness across d ∈D, and Lemma 4.14(b) gives X(ℓ†) 0 (πd) = x†, so πd ∈Π†. The conclusion we want is distinctness of zτj, not of zt⋆ 0. When t⋆ 0 = τj these coincide. When t⋆ 0 < τj, distinct zt⋆ 0 values could in principle produce coinciding zτj via chain collision after the exit step. We now count collision-inducing targets under the hypothesis s ≤√n/4. For d ∈D: Lemma 4.14 constructs πd by swapping values of π1 at u := zt⋆ 0−1(π1) and wd := π−1 1 (d) only. Write ∆:= τ j −t⋆ 0 ≤s−1. The chain of πd starting from d = zt⋆ 0(πd) follows π1’s iterations from d until it hits one of the two swapped positions. Call d ∈D clean if the π1-orbit d,π1(d),π2 1(d),...,π∆−1 1 (d) is disjoint from {u,wd}. For clean d, πd’s chain from d coincides with π1’s chain from d for the first ∆ iterations, yielding zτj(πd) = π∆ 1 (d), which is an injective function of d since π1 is a permutation. We bound |D \clean| by two contributions: (i) Orbit hits u: πr 1(d) = u for some r ∈[0,∆−1], i.e., d ∈{π−r 1 (u) : r ∈[0,∆−1]}, at most ∆≤s values. (ii) Orbit hits wd = π−1 1 (d): πr 1(d) = π−1 1 (d) for some r ∈[0,∆−1], i.e., πr+1 1 (d) = d, i.e., d lies in a π1-cycle of length dividing r +1 ∈[1,∆]. The number of such d ∈[n] is at most ∑ℓdivides some r+1∈[1,∆] ℓ· cℓ(π1) ≤∑ℓ≤∆ℓ·cℓ(π1) ≤∆·n ≤sn in worst case, where cℓ(π1) is the number of π1-cycles of length ℓ. This worst-case bound is too weak. To control (ii), we restrict attention to reference permutations π1 whose non-principal cycles all have length > s. For uniform π1 ∈Sn conditioned on Dchain (cycle of 1 has length > k), the probability of having no non-principal cycle of length ≤s is a positive density ρs > 0 (not uniform in s: ρs decreases as s grows, but positivity for each fixed s ≤√n/4 is what we need). By classical cycle-structure asymptotics (e.g., Arratia–Tavar´e), the number of cycles of each length ℓis approximately Poisson with mean 1/ℓfor ℓ≤n1/2, so the probability of having zero non-principal cycles of length ≤s satisfies ρs ≈∏s ℓ=1 e−1/ℓ≥(es)−1 for s = o(√n) (valid under our precondition s ≤√n/4). Thus a valid reference permutation π1 (one satisfying both the exit condition of Lemma 4.11 and the cycle-structure restriction) exists so long as the first-exit-step pigeonhole density 1/s combined with ρs ≥(es)−1 gives positive probability; this holds throughout the precondition regime. Once a valid π1 is fixed, neither the first-exit-step pigeonhole nor the cycle-structure restriction propagates into the distinct-value count of zτ j (see final paragraph of proof and Remark 4.15). Under this restriction, contribution (ii) vanishes (no short non-principal cycles), so |D \clean| ≤s. Hence |clean| ≥|D| −s ≥n/2 −k −s, and the injective map d 7→π∆ 1 (d) on clean d gives ≥n/2 −k −s distinct values of zτj(πd) on Π†. Note: the first-exit-step pigeonhole and cycle-structure restriction only reduce the 15 number of valid reference permutations π1; once a single valid π1 is fixed, the distinct-value count of zτj over the resulting {πd : d ∈clean} ⊆Π† is |clean| directly, with no 1/s factor. Thus the distinct-value count is ≥n/2−k −s ≥n/4 in the regime s ≤√n/4 and n ≥4k for n sufficiently large. For s > √n/4, the cycle-restriction argument degrades and the product bound is not guaranteed by this proof strategy; we fall back to Proposition 4.4, consistent with the regime condition of Conjecture 3.1. Remark 4.18 (Effect on Conjecture 3.1’s constant). The distinct-value count n/2 −k −s ≥n/4 gives a bandwidth factor of ⌈log2(n/4)/(Hmp)⌉= ⌈(log2 n −2)/(Hmp)⌉, matching Ω(log2 n/(Hmp)) up to an additive O(1) in the numerator. No logs loss appears in the distinct-value count. (The O(H logs) gap between Conjecture 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 is therefore attributable to the upper bound’s log2(2s) factor from windowed doubling’s inner loop, not to the lower-bound proof strategy.) In particular, Conjecture 3.1’s statement L = Ω(⌈k/s⌉·⌈logn/(Hmp)⌉) holds (modulo (⋆)) with a universal constant c in the regime s ≤√n/4. Lemma 4.19 (Per-window cost). For n ≥4k, s ≤√n/4, and a locality-respecting controller, any correct algorithm must satisfy ℓj −ℓj−1 ≥B−1 for every j ∈{1,...,W}, where B := log2 n Hmp  and ℓ0 := 0. More precisely, B′ := ℓj −ℓj−1 ≥⌈log2(⌊n/2⌋−k −s)/(Hmp)⌉≥⌈(log2 n −2)/(Hmp)⌉≥ B−1; the additive −1 is O(1) and is absorbed into the universal constant. No log2 s loss appears. Proof. By Lemma 4.17, in the regime s ≤√n/4 there exist ℓ† and x† such that zτj takes at least M := ⌊n/2⌋−k−s ≥n/4 distinct values on Π† := {π : X(ℓ†) 0 (π) = x†}, for n ≥n0 sufficiently large. In case j ≥2, ℓ† = ℓj−1; in case j = 1, ℓ† = 0 = ℓ0. A correct algorithm maps each distinct zτj to a distinct final query-token state (otherwise two inputs with different correct answers would produce the same output), so in particular at layer ℓj, the representation X(ℓj) 0 must take at least M distinct values on Π†. By Lemma 4.2 with starting state x† and B′ := ℓj −ℓ† layers of evolution, M ≤|ΣB′(x†)| ≤2B′Hmp, which gives B′ ≥⌈log2 M/(Hmp)⌉≥⌈(log2 n −2)/(Hmp)⌉≥B −1 where B := ⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉. Thus ℓj −ℓj−1 ≥B −1 (with ℓ0 = 0 for j = 1); the additive −1 is absorbed into the universal constant of Conjecture 3.1. Remark 4.20 (Dense regime n = O(k)). The adversarial construction of Lemma 4.17 requires n ≥4k. In the dense regime n = O(k), Proposition 4.4’s unconditional bound L ≥max(⌈(k −1)/s⌉,⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉) still applies, giving L = Ω(⌈k/s⌉). The strengthening from ⌈(k −1)/s⌉to ⌈k/s⌉via Lemma 4.16 applies in the same s ≤√n/4 regime as Conjecture 3.1 (since Lemma 4.16’s proof also invokes the clean-target counting of Lemma 4.17); in particular, this unit-level strengthening requires the cycle-structure restriction and is not claimed for general s. Proof strategy for Conjecture 3.1, modulo (⋆). Assuming the conclusion of Lemma 4.11 (exit exists at every window) and the regime n ≥4k, s ≤√n/4, we combine Lemma 4.16 and Lemma 4.19. Setting ℓ0 := 0, define ℓj as the earliest layer ℓ∈{0,1,...,L} such that |Tℓ(π)| ≥τj + 1 for every π in some non-empty set Π† j relevant to window j (the set of π satisfying Dchain and consistent with a fixed prefix state x† at the resolution layer of window j −1, as in Lemma 4.17). This definition makes ℓj well-defined for every j ≤W: since |TL(π)| ≥τj +1 for some π ∈Π† j is necessary for a correct algorithm (by Lemma 4.1 applied to that π’s chain through zτ j to the final output zk), the set of layers satisfying the condition is non-empty and contains L; its minimum is ℓj. Moreover, ℓj is monotone in j (since τ j is), so ℓ0 ≤ℓ1 ≤··· ≤ℓW ≤L. With this definition, 16 Lemma 4.16 gives ℓj ·s ≥τj (rephrased: at layer ℓj, |Tℓj| ≤1+ℓjs must be ≥τj +1, i.e., ℓj ·s ≥τj), and Lemma 4.19 gives ℓj −ℓj−1 ≥B−1 where B := ⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉. Now combine: (a) Sequentiality (Lemma 4.16): ℓj ·s ≥τj for each j; in particular ℓW ≥W when τW = k. (b) Per-window cost (Lemma 4.19): ℓj−ℓj−1 ≥B−1 for each j ∈{1,...,W}, where B := ⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉. Summing (b) over j = 1,...,W with ℓ0 = 0 gives L ≥ℓW = W ∑ j=1 (ℓj −ℓj−1) ≥W ·(B−1) ≥ k s  · log2 n Hmp  ·  1−1 B  . For n ≥n0 := 100 (which ensures √n/4 ≤n/16, hence k +s ≤n/4+n/16 ≤n/3, hence ⌊n/2⌋−k −s ≥ n/6 ≥n0/6 ≥16) and log2 n > Hmp (so that B ≥2; this is the condition for the bandwidth factor to be non-trivial—otherwise ⌈log2 n/(Hmp)⌉= 1 and the product bound reduces to cache reachability, already covered by Proposition 4.4), we have 1−1/B ≥1/2, yielding L ≥1 2 · k s  · log2 n Hmp  , i.e., Conjecture 3.1’s statement with c = 1/2. For s > √n/4, the product-structure statement is not guaranteed by this proof strategy and we fall back to Proposition 4.4’s max-bound, as stipulated in the Conjecture 3.1 precondition. The derivation uses the conclusion of Lemma 4.11 (existence of exit at every window); since Lemma 4.11 is left as Open Problem 1, this establishes Conjecture 3.1 modulo (⋆) rather than unconditionally. No log2 s loss appears in this derivation: the clean-target counting of Lemma 4.17 reduces the set of valid reference permutations (via first-exit-step pigeonhole and cycle-structure restriction), but once a single valid π1 is fixed, the distinct-value count of zτj is Ω(n) directly. Why the product (not max) bound holds. The fact that each of the W windows requires B layers between resolutions, not just total, is what gives the product W ·B rather than max(W,B). Lemma 4.19 is essential: window j’s resolution cannot be pipelined within the B layers devoted to window j−1, because to distinguish among the Ω(n/s) possible values of zτ j requires B fresh layers past ℓj−1. 4.4 Remarks on Theorem 3.6 The proof of Theorem 3.6 was given in Section 3 following the theorem statement. Here we collect additional commentary on the barrier and the search for bypasses. Remark 4.21 (Breaking the barrier). Theorem 3.6 does not rule out lower bounds outside the class of Definition 3.5. Three natural avenues remain: 1. Unconditional communication-complexity lower bounds: the pointer chasing problem has been studied in multi-party communication models. Nisan and Wigderson (1993) proved a ˜Ω(n/k2) lower bound on the k-round randomized communication complexity of k-hop pointer chasing; (Yehudayoff, 2020) improved this to ˜Ω(n/k −klogn), essentially matching Klauck’s earlier randomized lower bound and tight up to the klogn additive term relative to Nisan-Wigderson’s ˜O(n/k) upper bound; (Mao et al., 2025) closed the remaining logn gap to Ω(n/k +k) via gadgetless lifting. These CC bounds are unconditional. What remains open is the lifting: translating such a CC bound into an unconditional Cache-Transformer depth lower bound without invoking (Sanford et al., 2024)’s MPC-conjecture reduction, which would bypass Definition 3.5. 17 2. Joint-state arguments: counting over the joint state (X(ℓ) i )i∈S(ℓ) rather than the single state X(ℓ) 0 . The joint state has capacity smp > Hmp whenever s > H, potentially escaping the per-layer bandwidth limit. However, in the PCn,k task only X(L) 0 decodes to the output, so arguments coupling to joint cache states require additional task structure (e.g., requiring multiple output tokens or global statistics). 3. Information-theoretic multi-output tasks: tasks whose output is Ω(Hmp) bits (e.g., requiring Ω(Hmp/logn) chain values as simultaneous outputs at layer L) can give L ≥log(output)/(Hmp) = Ω(1) from informa- tion alone. Such tasks deviate from PCn,k as stated, but may be natural models of multi-hop reasoning in different settings. Each of these is a distinct open problem. Remark 4.22 (Exhaustive search for bypass arguments). We considered eight alternative approaches to escape the barrier within counting-based arguments: (i) asymmetric rounds (parallel reasoning at multiple token positions); (ii) adversarial online streaming (cache commitment before seeing the rest of the input); (iii) query/cache capacity asymmetry (smp vs mp); (iv) counting distinguishability at non-query tokens; (v) pebbling-game time-space tradeoffs; (vi) direct-sum and XOR lemmas for independent task instances; (vii) communication-complexity fooling-set arguments, in particular round-elimination lower bounds for pointer chasing of the Duris et al. (1987) style; and (viii) average-case (random-π) hardness. Each approach either saturates at ⌈k/s⌉or yields a strictly weaker bound (e.g., (vii) via unconditional MPC bounds gives logk/s, weaker than k/s for k ≥2). We omit the detailed case-by-case analysis; the unified conclusion is the per-window structural limit formalized by Theorem 3.6. 4.5 Proof sketch of Theorem 3.2 Serial construction. At each of k outer layers, the query token performs a single content-based lookup: its current slot encodes the current zt, keys encode token identities i, values encode payloads π(i), and the attention output becomes the next zt+1. Implementing one lookup requires O(⌈log2 n/(mp)⌉) sub-layers to encode/decode n-ary pointers in m-dimensional p-bit vectors (standard construction, e.g., via binary decomposition). Total: L = O(k⌈log2 n/(mp)⌉). Windowed pointer doubling. Partition the chain into W = ⌈k/s⌉windows of size s each. Within each window, apply pointer doubling: at stage t, compute π2t on the window’s starting token using ⌈log2 s⌉stages, so window j consumes O(⌈log2 s⌉· ⌈log2 n/(mp)⌉) layers. Total: L = O(⌈k/s⌉· ⌈log2 s⌉· ⌈log2 n/(mp)⌉). Take the minimum of the serial and windowed costs. 4.6 Proofs of Theorems 3.8, 3.9, and Corollary 3.10 Fix π ∼Unif(Sn). Let zt := πt(1) for t ≥0, with z0 = 1. The cache at stage t is Ct ⊆[n], |Ct| = s. Success at stage t is Et := {zt ∈Ct}, joint success is E := ∩T t=1Et. 4.6.1 Oblivious case: exponential decay (Theorem 3.8) The key idea is direct enumeration of (z1,...,zT) trajectories rather than a chain-rule decomposition. Because π is uniform, any fixed distinct trajectory has probability (n −T)!/n!; the joint hit event is a union over trajectories lying in C1 ×···×CT. Proof of Theorem 3.8. Condition on the caches C1,...,CT (oblivious, so independent of π). Define A :=   (a1,...,aT) ∈C1 ×···×CT : a1,...,aT distinct, at ̸= 1 for all t . Trivially |A| ≤sT. 18 For any (a1,...,aT) ∈A, Pr[z1 = a1,...,zT = aT] is the probability that π(1) = a1, π(a1) = a2, ..., π(aT−1) = aT. These are T consistent constraints on the bijection π (consistent because 1,a1,...,aT are distinct), giving probability (n−T)!/n! = 1/(n(n−1)···(n−T +1)). Let E good := {z1,...,zT distinct, all ̸= 1}∩E and E bad := E \E good. Then Pr[E good | {Ct}] = |A| n(n−1)···(n−T +1) ≤ sT (n−T +1)T ≤  s n−T T , using n(n−1)···(n−T +1) ≥(n−T +1)T ≥(n−T)T for n ≥T +1. For E bad: this requires zi = z j for some i < j, or zi = 1 for some i. For the first, zi = z j ⇐⇒π j−i(zi) = zi; by exchangeability of uniform permutations, Pr[πm(y) = y] for any fixed y equals the probability that y lies in a cycle of length dividing m, which is at most m/n. Hence Pr[zi = zj] ≤(j −i)/n. Summing with exact value: ∑1≤i<j≤T(j−i) = ∑T−1 d=1 d(T −d) = T(T −1)(T +1)/6, giving ∑i<j Pr[zi = zj] ≤T(T 2−1)/(6n) ≤T 3/(6n). For the second, Pr[zi = 1] = Pr[πi(1) = 1] ≤i/n; summing ∑T i=1 i/n = T(T +1)/(2n) ≤T 2/n. In total, Pr[E bad] ≤T 3 6n + T 2 n ≤T 3 +6T 2 6n ≤T 3 3n (T ≥6), with a trivial explicit bound for T ≤5. Using the uniform bound Pr[E bad] ≤2T 3/n (valid for all T ≥1) for simplicity, we combine: Pr[E ] = Pr[E good]+Pr[E bad] ≤  s n−T T + 2T 3 n . (A sharper wrapper constant T 3/(3n)+ lower-order replaces 2T 3/n for T ≥6, but does not change the asymptotic character of the bound.) Taking expectation over {Ct} (which has no π-dependence) preserves the bound. 4.6.2 Adaptive case: upper bound (Theorem 3.9, upper bound) The upper bound uses the fact that at stage 1, any locality-respecting controller’s cache C1 is independent of π(1) (because C1 reads only positions in T0 = {0}, which is disjoint from π’s domain [n]). Proof of Theorem 3.9, upper bound. At stage 1, the trace T0 = {0} (only the query token is accessible before any attention computation), so π|T0 is vacuous. Hence C1 is a deterministic function of the empty string, i.e., constant. Therefore C1 ⊥π, and since E ⊆E1 = {π(1) ∈C1}, Pr[E ] ≤Pr[π(1) ∈C1] = |C1| n ≤s n ≤ s n−T , using that π(1) is uniform on [n] under π ∼Unif(Sn), |C1| ≤s, and n ≥T +1. 4.6.3 Adaptive case: matching achievability (Theorem 3.9, lower bound) We exhibit an explicit locality-respecting adaptive controller whose joint success probability matches the upper bound s/n of Theorem 3.9 with exact equality. The strategy is “chain-tracking”: at stage 1, gamble s −1 random slots plus the query’s source position 1 to learn π(1); after a hit, the subsequent chain is deterministically known to the controller. Proof of Theorem 3.9, lower bound. Define the controller A⋆as follows: • Stage 1: C1 = {1,a1,a2,...,as−1} for any fixed distinct ai ∈[n]\{1}. Note 1 ∈C1. 19 • Stage t ≥2: If E1,...,Et−1 all occurred (stage t −1 cache Ct−1 ∋zt−1), then by induction z1,...,zt−1 ∈ Tt−1, and controller has read π at these positions. In particular, controller knows zt = π(zt−1). Set Ct ∋zt (other s−1 slots arbitrary). • If any earlier Ei failed, set Ct arbitrarily (the joint event E has already failed). Locality check: At stage t ≥2, Ct depends only on π|{1,z1,...,zt−1} ⊆π|Tt−1 (all indices in prior caches). Locality holds. Success probability: Under A⋆, if π(1) ∈C1 then stage 1 succeeds (z1 = π(1) ∈C1 = E1), and the chain-tracking induction at subsequent stages deterministically maintains zt ∈Ct for all t ≥2: the controller has read π at all previously-visited chain positions (including zt−1), so it knows zt = π(zt−1) and places it in Ct. This covers the fixed-point case π(1) = 1 as well: if π(1) = 1, the controller reads this at stage 1, knows zt = 1 for all t, and includes 1 in every Ct, so E holds. Therefore Pr[E ] ≥Pr[π(1) ∈C1] = |C1| n = s n. This matches the upper bound s/n of Theorem 3.9 exactly (both bounds are s/n), giving Pr[E ] = s/n. Proof of Corollary 3.10. Adaptive achieves Pr[E ] ≥s/n (Theorem 3.9, achievability), while oblivious gives Pr[E ] ≤(s/(n −T))T + 2T 3/n. In the regime sT ≥2T 3nT−1 (equivalently (s/n)T ≥2T 3/n), the main term dominates the error term: since (s/(n −T))T ≥(s/n)T ≥2T 3/n, we have (s/(n −T))T + 2T 3/n ≤ 2·(s/(n−T))T. To relate (s/(n −T))T to (s/n)T, note s/(n −T) = (s/n) · n/(n −T) = (s/n) · (1 + T/(n −T)), so (s/(n −T))T = (s/n)T · (1 + T/(n −T))T. Under the mild condition T ≤√n/2 (so, using n ≥4 to get n −T ≥n/2, we have (1 + T/(n −T))T ≤eT 2/(n−T) ≤e(n/4)/(n/2) = e1/2 ≤2), we have (s/(n −T))T ≤ 2(s/n)T. Hence s/n (s/(n−T))T +2T 3/n ≥ s/n 4·(s/n)T = (n/s)T−1 4 = Ω �(n/s)T−1 . Outside this regime (when 2T 3/n dominates), the ratio is ≥(s/n) ÷ (4T 3/n) = Ω(s/T 3), which, while weaker than (n/s)T−1, still separates the two asymptotically for s ≫T 3. 5 Architecture Comparison Full attention’s depth scales as Ω(k/s) to O((k/s)logs), with a gap of O(logs). Linear attention and RNNs are stuck at Θ(k) regardless of cache. Full attention’s advantage depends critically on cache size. 6 Experiments We validate our bounds via constructive verification: we hardcode the serial pointer-chasing algorithm directly into Transformer weights and measure accuracy on random permutations. This eliminates optimization confounds entirely—success or failure reflects theoretical possibility, not SGD’s ability to find a solution. 6.1 Hardcoded Transformer (serial strategy) We implement the serial strategy from Theorem 3.2’s proof as explicit Transformer weights on PC16,k.
